nativeRange.habitat: Muddy and sandy bottoms of inshore bays, lagoons, and sandy-rubble flats; typically found at depths of 3–25 m; uses burrows for refuge

diet.notes: Feeds by sifting mouthfuls of sand to extract small invertebrates, organic matter, and microorganisms from the substrate. In aquaria, has been reported to accept sinking pellets, mysis shrimp, and finely chopped frozen foods. A live sand bed with a healthy microfauna community is generally advised to supplement its natural sifting behavior. Continuous sand-sifting is typically observed throughout the day.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo credit: Brett, O.J. / www.tropicalfavourites.com. Thumbnail; full-resolution image access may require navigating FishBase directly.

summary: A small sand-sifting goby from the Western Central Pacific that spends much of its time filtering sand for food. Generally considered intermediate-level due to dietary and substrate needs.
careNotes:
The Orangemarked Goby (also traded as Orange-striped Goby or Pink-lined Goby) is a sand-sifting species from inshore bays and lagoons of the Western Central Pacific, ranging from the Philippines to New Caledonia and throughout Micronesia. FishBase notes it as tolerant of both marine and brackish conditions in the wild, though standard full-marine salinity (SG 1.020–1.026) is generally advised for aquarium keeping.

A sand substrate of at least 5–10 cm depth is widely considered important, as this species is documented using burrows for refuge and is observed constantly sifting the sand bed for food. A tank footprint of at least 60 cm length is generally advised to give the fish adequate foraging territory. A minimum volume of 100 L is recommended, erring on the cautious side to provide sufficient sand area and stable water quality.

This goby tends to be peaceful toward most tank mates but has been known to be territorial with conspecifics and similar-looking sand-sifting gobies. Keeping a mated pair is possible in larger tanks, but two males in close quarters may lead to aggression. Compatible with most reef inhabitants; generally considered reef-safe as it does not bother corals.

Feeding may require some patience at first: while many individuals gradually accept prepared and frozen foods, consistent target feeding with sinking foods is often needed to ensure adequate nutrition beyond what the sand bed provides. A thriving, live sand bed with copepods, amphipods, and small worms is generally considered beneficial for the long-term health of this species.

Escape risk is moderate to high for gobies generally; a lid or cover is generally advised, particularly for tanks with low rockwork walls.

Temperature note: FishBase records a wild range of 22–30°C with a preferred mean of ~28°C. The upper value of 29°C used here reflects the preferred range; the full 30°C upper limit is possible but represents the warm edge of the wild habitat.

Size note: FishBase records a maximum of 9.5 cm SL (standard length); total length is typically slightly larger. The adultSizeCm range of 6–10 cm reflects the typical aquarium size range for this species.
breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented for this species in the aquarium hobby. As with many sand-dwelling gobies, breeding behavior in captivity has occasionally been reported in pairs, but details are sparse in available sources.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Attempted to locate a species-specific product page for Amblygobius decussatus on LiveAquaria via the gobies collection and site search; this species does not appear to be currently listed. The gobies collection URL was the designated primary source URL. Secondary sources were used for all data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary data source used for this entry. Confirmed scientific name, max size (9.5 cm SL), temperature range (22–30°C, preferred 26.3–29°C), native range (Western Central Pacific: Philippines to New Caledonia, Yaeyama Islands, Great Barrier Reef, Micronesia), habitat (marine/brackish; muddy bottoms of inshore bays and lagoons, 3–25 m), diet (sand-sifter, small invertebrates and organic matter), and taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes). FishBase image credit: Brett, O.J. / www.tropicalfavourites.com.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), max size (9.5 cm SL), habitat (muddy substrates, uses burrows), and distribution (western Pacific). Confirmed common name Orange-striped goby. Used for taxonomy and distribution cross-verification.

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented for this species. Occasional reports of spawning in captive pairs exist but detailed protocols are not available in major hobby sources.
